question:
Problem A-4

Let \( k \) be a positive integer and let \( m=6 k-1 \). Let
\[
S(m)=\sum_{j=1}^{2 k-1}(-1)^{j+1}\binom{m}{3 j-1}
\]

For example with \( k=3 \),
\[
S(17)=\binom{17}{2}-\binom{17}{5}+\binom{17}{8}-\binom{17}{11}+\binom{17}{14} .
\]

Prove that \( S(m) \) is never zero. \( \left[\right. \) As usual, \( \binom{m}{r}=\frac{m!}{r!(m-r)!} \).]
solution:
A-4.
Let \( \binom{m}{r}=0 \) for \( r>m \) and for \( r<0 \). For \( i=0,1,2 \) let
\[
T_{i}(m)=\binom{m}{i}-\binom{m}{i+3}+\binom{m}{i+6}-\binom{m}{i+9}+\cdots .
\]

We note that \( S(m)=T_{2}(m)+1 \). Since \( \binom{n}{r}=\binom{n-1}{r}+\binom{n-1}{r-1} \),
\[
\begin{array}{l}
T_{2}(m)=T_{2}(m-1)+T_{1}(m-1), T_{1}(m)=T_{1}(m-1)+T_{0}(m-1) \\
T_{0}(m)=T_{0}(m-1)-T_{2}(m-1)
\end{array}
\]

Let the backwards difference operator \( \nabla \) be given by \( \nabla f(n)=f(n)-f(n-1) \). Then
\[
\nabla T_{2}(m)=T_{1}(m-1), \nabla T_{1}(m)=T_{0}(m-1), \nabla T_{0}(m)=-T_{2}(m-1)
\]

These imply that
\[
\nabla^{3} T_{2}(m)=\nabla^{2} T_{1}(m-1)=\nabla T_{0}(m-2)=-T_{2}(m-3) \text { for } m \geqslant 3 .
\]

Expanding \( \nabla^{3} T_{2}(m) \), this gives us
\[
T_{2}(m)=3\left[T_{2}(m-1)-T_{2}(m-2)\right] \text { for } m \geqslant 3 .
\]

When \( m=6 k-1 \) with \( k \geqslant 1 \), we have \( m \geqslant 5 \). It then follows from \( (\mathrm{R}) \) that \( T_{2}(m) \equiv 0(\bmod 3) \) and hence \( S(m) \equiv 1(\bmod 3) \). Thus \( S(m) \neq 0 \).

descriptive_long.question:
Problem A-4

Let multiplindex be a positive integer and let derivedint=6 multiplindex-1. Let
\[
seriesfunc(derivedint)=\sum_{loopingidx=1}^{2 multiplindex-1}(-1)^{loopingidx+1}\binom{derivedint}{3 loopingidx-1}
\]

For example with multiplindex=3,
\[
seriesfunc(17)=\binom{17}{2}-\binom{17}{5}+\binom{17}{8}-\binom{17}{11}+\binom{17}{14} .
\]

Prove that seriesfunc(derivedint) is never zero. [As usual, \(\binom{derivedint}{chooserank}=\frac{derivedint!}{chooserank!(derivedint-chooserank)!}\).]
descriptive_long.solution:
A-4.
Let \( \binom{derivedint}{chooserank}=0 \) for chooserank>derivedint and for chooserank<0. For trinaryidx=0,1,2 let
\[
altseries_{trinaryidx}(derivedint)=\binom{derivedint}{trinaryidx}-\binom{derivedint}{trinaryidx+3}+\binom{derivedint}{trinaryidx+6}-\binom{derivedint}{trinaryidx+9}+\cdots .
\]

We note that \( seriesfunc(derivedint)=altseriestwo(derivedint)+1 \). Since \( \binom{generalnum}{chooserank}=\binom{generalnum-1}{chooserank}+\binom{generalnum-1}{chooserank-1} \),
\[
\begin{array}{l}
altseriestwo(derivedint)=altseriestwo(derivedint-1)+altseriesone(derivedint-1),\quad altseriesone(derivedint)=altseriesone(derivedint-1)+altserieszero(derivedint-1) \\
altserieszero(derivedint)=altserieszero(derivedint-1)-altseriestwo(derivedint-1)
\end{array}
\]

Let the backwards difference operator \( \nabla \) be given by \( \nabla f(generalnum)=f(generalnum)-f(generalnum-1) \). Then
\[
\nabla altseriestwo(derivedint)=altseriesone(derivedint-1),\quad \nabla altseriesone(derivedint)=altserieszero(derivedint-1),\quad \nabla altserieszero(derivedint)=-altseriestwo(derivedint-1)
\]

These imply that
\[
\nabla^{3} altseriestwo(derivedint)=\nabla^{2} altseriesone(derivedint-1)=\nabla altserieszero(derivedint-2)=-altseriestwo(derivedint-3) \text { for } derivedint \geqslant 3 .
\]

Expanding \( \nabla^{3} altseriestwo(derivedint) \), this gives us
\[
altseriestwo(derivedint)=3\left[altseriestwo(derivedint-1)-altseriestwo(derivedint-2)\right] \text { for } derivedint \geqslant 3 . \tag{R}
\]

When derivedint=6 multiplindex-1 with multiplindex \geqslant 1, we have derivedint \geqslant 5. It then follows from (R) that altseriestwo(derivedint) \equiv 0 (\bmod 3) and hence seriesfunc(derivedint) \equiv 1 (\bmod 3). Thus seriesfunc(derivedint) \neq 0.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem A-4

Let \( hazelnut \) be a positive integer and let \( giraffes=6 hazelnut-1 \). Let
\[
waterfall(giraffes)=\sum_{opossums=1}^{2 hazelnut-1}(-1)^{opossums+1}\binom{giraffes}{3 opossums-1}
\]

For example with \( hazelnut=3 \),
\[
waterfall(17)=\binom{17}{2}-\binom{17}{5}+\binom{17}{8}-\binom{17}{11}+\binom{17}{14} .
\]

Prove that \( waterfall(giraffes) \) is never zero. \( \left[\right. \) As usual, \( \binom{giraffes}{butterfly}=\frac{giraffes!}{butterfly!(giraffes-butterfly)!} \).]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-4.
Let \( \binom{giraffes}{butterfly}=0 \) for \( butterfly>giraffes \) and for \( butterfly<0 \). For \( pinecone=0,1,2 \) let
\[
strawhats(giraffes)=\binom{giraffes}{pinecone}-\binom{giraffes}{pinecone+3}+\binom{giraffes}{pinecone+6}-\binom{giraffes}{pinecone+9}+\cdots .
\]

We note that \( waterfall(giraffes)=drumstick(giraffes)+1 \). Since \( \binom{marigold}{butterfly}=\binom{marigold-1}{butterfly}+\binom{marigold-1}{butterfly-1} \),
\[
\begin{array}{l}
drumstick(giraffes)=drumstick(giraffes-1)+paintings(giraffes-1), paintings(giraffes)=paintings(giraffes-1)+lighthouse(giraffes-1) \\
lighthouse(giraffes)=lighthouse(giraffes-1)-drumstick(giraffes-1)
\end{array}
\]

Let the backwards difference operator \( \nabla \) be given by \( \nabla f(marigold)=f(marigold)-f(marigold-1) \). Then
\[
\nabla drumstick(giraffes)=paintings(giraffes-1), \nabla paintings(giraffes)=lighthouse(giraffes-1), \nabla lighthouse(giraffes)=-drumstick(giraffes-1)
\]

These imply that
\[
\nabla^{3} drumstick(giraffes)=\nabla^{2} paintings(giraffes-1)=\nabla lighthouse(giraffes-2)=-drumstick(giraffes-3) \text { for } giraffes \geqslant 3 .
\]

Expanding \( \nabla^{3} drumstick(giraffes) \), this gives us
\[
drumstick(giraffes)=3\left[drumstick(giraffes-1)-drumstick(giraffes-2)\right] \text { for } giraffes \geqslant 3 .
\]

When \( giraffes=6 hazelnut-1 \) with \( hazelnut \geqslant 1 \), we have \( giraffes \geqslant 5 \). It then follows from \( (\mathrm{R}) \) that \( drumstick(giraffes) \equiv 0(\bmod 3) \) and hence \( waterfall(giraffes) \equiv 1(\bmod 3) \). Thus \( waterfall(giraffes) \neq 0 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem A-4

Let \( infiniteidx \) be a positive integer and let \( fractionalval = 6\, infiniteidx - 1 \). Let
\[
voidprod(fractionalval)=\sum_{stillpoint=1}^{2\, infiniteidx -1}(-1)^{\, stillpoint +1}\binom{fractionalval}{3\, stillpoint -1}
\]

For example with \( infiniteidx = 3 \),
\[
voidprod(17)=\binom{17}{2}-\binom{17}{5}+\binom{17}{8}-\binom{17}{11}+\binom{17}{14} .
\]

Prove that \( voidprod(fractionalval) \) is never zero. \( \left[\right. \) As usual, \( \binom{fractionalval}{wholeamt}=\frac{fractionalval!}{wholeamt!(fractionalval-wholeamt)!} \).]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-4.
Let \( \binom{fractionalval}{wholeamt}=0 \) for \( wholeamt>fractionalval \) and for \( wholeamt<0 \). For \( constantval=0,1,2 \) let
\[
steadfasti(fractionalval)=\binom{fractionalval}{constantval}-\binom{fractionalval}{constantval+3}+\binom{fractionalval}{constantval+6}-\binom{fractionalval}{constantval+9}+\cdots .
\]

We note that \( voidprod(fractionalval)=steadfasttwo(fractionalval)+1 \). Since \( \binom{fixedbound}{wholeamt}=\binom{fixedbound-1}{wholeamt}+\binom{fixedbound-1}{wholeamt-1} \),
\[
\begin{array}{l}
steadfasttwo(fractionalval)=steadfasttwo(fractionalval-1)+steadfastone(fractionalval-1),\quad steadfastone(fractionalval)=steadfastone(fractionalval-1)+steadfastzero(fractionalval-1) \\
steadfastzero(fractionalval)=steadfastzero(fractionalval-1)-steadfasttwo(fractionalval-1)
\end{array}
\]

Let the backwards difference operator \( \nabla \) be given by \( \nabla f(fixedbound)=f(fixedbound)-f(fixedbound-1) \). Then
\[
\nabla steadfasttwo(fractionalval)=steadfastone(fractionalval-1),\quad \nabla steadfastone(fractionalval)=steadfastzero(fractionalval-1),\quad \nabla steadfastzero(fractionalval)=-steadfasttwo(fractionalval-1)
\]

These imply that
\[
\nabla^{3} steadfasttwo(fractionalval)=\nabla^{2} steadfastone(fractionalval-1)=\nabla steadfastzero(fractionalval-2)=-steadfasttwo(fractionalval-3) \text { for } fractionalval \geqslant 3 .
\]

Expanding \( \nabla^{3} steadfasttwo(fractionalval) \), this gives us
\[
steadfasttwo(fractionalval)=3\left[ steadfasttwo(fractionalval-1)-steadfasttwo(fractionalval-2) \right] \text { for } fractionalval \geqslant 3 .
\]

When \( fractionalval = 6\, infiniteidx - 1 \) with \( infiniteidx \geqslant 1 \), we have \( fractionalval \geqslant 5 \). It then follows from \( (\mathrm{R}) \) that \( steadfasttwo(fractionalval) \equiv 0(\bmod 3) \) and hence \( voidprod(fractionalval) \equiv 1(\bmod 3) \). Thus \( voidprod(fractionalval) \neq 0 \).

garbled_string.question:
Problem A-4

Let \( ouveplix \) be a positive integer and let \( viburtoz=6 ouveplix-1 \). Let
\[
tobjarnik(viburtoz)=\sum_{caxendot=1}^{2 ouveplix-1}(-1)^{caxendot+1}\binom{viburtoz}{3 caxendot-1}
\]

For example with \( ouveplix=3 \),
\[
tobjarnik(17)=\binom{17}{2}-\binom{17}{5}+\binom{17}{8}-\binom{17}{11}+\binom{17}{14} .
\]

Prove that \( tobjarnik(viburtoz) \) is never zero. \( \left[\right. \) As usual, \( \binom{viburtoz}{qimazulo}=\frac{viburtoz!}{qimazulo!(viburtoz-qimazulo)!} \).]
garbled_string.solution:
A-4.
Let \( \binom{viburtoz}{qimazulo}=0 \) for \( qimazulo>viburtoz \) and for \( qimazulo<0 \). For \( glomertx=0,1,2 \) let
\[
lavumpek(viburtoz)=\binom{viburtoz}{glomertx}-\binom{viburtoz}{glomertx+3}+\binom{viburtoz}{glomertx+6}-\binom{viburtoz}{glomertx+9}+\cdots .
\]

We note that \( tobjarnik(viburtoz)=vivnargel(viburtoz)+1 \). Since \( \binom{pewdarin}{qimazulo}=\binom{pewdarin-1}{qimazulo}+\binom{pewdarin-1}{qimazulo-1} \),
\[
\begin{array}{l}
vivnargel(viburtoz)=vivnargel(viburtoz-1)+qerdoniv(viburtoz-1), qerdoniv(viburtoz)=qerdoniv(viburtoz-1)+ysmaltex(viburtoz-1) \\
ysmaltex(viburtoz)=ysmaltex(viburtoz-1)-vivnargel(viburtoz-1)
\end{array}
\]

Let the backwards difference operator \( \nabla \) be given by \( \nabla f(pewdarin)=f(pewdarin)-f(pewdarin-1) \). Then
\[
\nabla vivnargel(viburtoz)=qerdoniv(viburtoz-1), \nabla qerdoniv(viburtoz)=ysmaltex(viburtoz-1), \nabla ysmaltex(viburtoz)=-vivnargel(viburtoz-1)
\]

These imply that
\[
\nabla^{3} vivnargel(viburtoz)=\nabla^{2} qerdoniv(viburtoz-1)=\nabla ysmaltex(viburtoz-2)=-vivnargel(viburtoz-3) \text { for } viburtoz \geqslant 3 .
\]

Expanding \( \nabla^{3} vivnargel(viburtoz) \), this gives us
\[
vivnargel(viburtoz)=3\left[vivnargel(viburtoz-1)-vivnargel(viburtoz-2)\right] \text { for } viburtoz \geqslant 3 .
\]

When \( viburtoz=6 ouveplix-1 \) with \( ouveplix \geqslant 1 \), we have \( viburtoz \geqslant 5 \). It then follows from \( (\mathrm{R}) \) that \( vivnargel(viburtoz) \equiv 0(\bmod 3) \) and hence \( tobjarnik(viburtoz) \equiv 1(\bmod 3) \). Thus \( tobjarnik(viburtoz) \neq 0 .

kernel_variant.question:
Let k be a positive integer and put n = 6k-1.  Define
\[
P(n)=\sum_{j=0}^{2k-2}(-1)^{j}\binom{n}{3j+2}.
\]
For example, for k=4 one has n=23 and
\[
P(23)=\binom{23}{2}-\binom{23}{5}+\binom{23}{8}-\binom{23}{11}+\binom{23}{14}-\binom{23}{17}+\binom{23}{20}.
\]
(Prove that P(n) is never zero.  Throughout we agree that \(\binom{p}{q}=0\) whenever \(q\notin\{0,1,\dots ,p\}\).)
kernel_variant.solution:
Introduce, for i = 0,1,2,

T_i(m)=\binom{m}{i}-\binom{m}{i+3}+\binom{m}{i+6}-\binom{m}{i+9}+\cdots \quad(m\in\mathbb N).

Because 2\equiv i\pmod3 when i=2, all summands occurring in P(n) lie in T_2(n).  More precisely, the largest index appearing in P(n) is 3(2k-2)+2=n-3, so

T_2(n)=P(n)-\binom{n}{n}=P(n)-1,\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad P(n)=T_2(n)+1.  (1)

Pascal's identity \(\binom{m}{r}=\binom{m-1}{r}+\binom{m-1}{r-1}\) yields

T_2(m)=T_2(m-1)+T_1(m-1),\quad T_1(m)=T_1(m-1)+T_0(m-1),\quad T_0(m)=T_0(m-1)-T_2(m-1).

Write the backward-difference operator \(\nabla f(m)=f(m)-f(m-1)\).  Then

\nabla T_2(m)=T_1(m-1),\quad \nabla T_1(m)=T_0(m-1),\quad \nabla T_0(m)=-T_2(m-1).

Repeated application gives, for m\ge3,

\nabla^{3}T_2(m)=\nabla^{2}T_1(m-1)=\nabla T_0(m-2)=-T_2(m-3).

Expanding \(\nabla^{3}T_2(m)\) one obtains the linear recurrence

T_2(m)=3\bigl[T_2(m-1)-T_2(m-2)\bigr]\quad(m\ge3).  (2)

Because n=6k-1\ge5, repeated use of (2) shows that

T_2(n)\equiv0\pmod3.  (3)

Finally, combining (1) and (3) gives

P(n)=T_2(n)+1\equiv1\pmod3\neq0.

Hence P(n) is nonzero for every positive integer k, as required.

core_steps: Partition the alternating sum by residues mod 3, defining the auxiliary sequences T₀, T₁, T₂ with S(m)=T₂(m)+1.
Apply Pascal’s identity to relate T₀, T₁, T₂ and rewrite those relations with the backward-difference operator ∇.
Take a third backward difference to obtain the linear recurrence  T₂(m)=3[T₂(m−1)−T₂(m−2)].
Use the recurrence modulo 3 and the fact m≡2 (mod 3) (because m=6k−1) to deduce 3|T₂(m), hence S(m)=T₂(m)+1≡1 (mod 3)≠0.

slot1.description: The illustrative choice of k in the sample computation (only pedagogical, not used in the proof).

slot2.description: Stated convention that \(\binom{m}{r}=0\) for r<0 or r>m; any equivalent convention giving the same zero values would work.
